Back to Tools

Prescription Drug Assistance Finder

Search a medication to find grants, foundations, and programs that may lower your costs.

On Medicare? You generally cannot use manufacturer copay coupons or savings cards — federal anti-kickback rules block them. But manufacturer Patient Assistance Programs (free drug by income) and nonprofit foundation grants still can help. This finder focuses on the programs that work with Medicare.

Try:

This finder is an educational starting point, not an application or eligibility decision. Program names, eligibility rules, funding, and contact details change often, and disease funds open and close based on availability. Always confirm directly with each program. Every legitimate program here is free to apply for — never pay a fee to enroll.

Do I Qualify? Quick Eligibility Estimator

Answer a few quick questions for a rough idea of which kinds of help you may qualify for. Nothing is saved or submitted.

How Drug Assistance Works on Medicare

Medicare Extra Help (Low-Income Subsidy)

A federal program that lowers or eliminates Part D premiums, deductibles, and copays. Apply free through Social Security — many people who qualify never sign up.

Nonprofit copay grant foundations

Charities like the PAN Foundation and HealthWell give grants for out-of-pocket drug costs for specific diseases. Unlike copay coupons, these can be used with Medicare.

Manufacturer patient assistance programs

Drug makers provide their medication free or low-cost to people who qualify by income. These are different from copay cards and often do serve Medicare beneficiaries.

Why copay cards usually don't work on Medicare

Federal anti-kickback law generally blocks people with Medicare from using manufacturer copay coupons — so foundations and patient assistance programs are the routes that apply to you.

The $2,000 Part D cap

Since 2025, what you pay out of pocket for covered drugs is capped at $2,000 a year, and you can spread it over the year with the Medicare Prescription Payment Plan.

Foundation Fund Status

Fund availability changes frequently and varies by diagnosis. These are general indicators only — always verify directly with the foundation before applying.

Documents You'll Need to Apply

Prescription Drug Assistance — Document Checklist

AltaMedicare.com · Bret Swope, licensed Utah Medicare agent · (801) 368-0700

  • Medicare card (red, white & blue)
  • Prescription drug plan (Part D) card
  • Social Security award or benefit letter
  • Proof of income (Social Security, pension, wages)
  • Most recent tax return, if you file one
  • Complete medication list with dosages
  • Preferred pharmacy name & address
  • Bank & resource information (for Extra Help and Medicaid)
  • Prescribing doctor's name & contact info (for diagnosis verification)

Struggling to afford a medication?

Send Bret your drug list — he'll check Extra Help, the right foundation, and which Part D plan covers your meds for the least. Free, no pressure.

Call (801) 368-0700 Text Your Meds
Free Medicare Review

Frequently Asked Questions

What is Medicare Extra Help?
Extra Help — the Part D Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) — lowers or eliminates your Part D premiums, deductible, and copays if you have limited income and resources. You apply free through Social Security at ssa.gov.
Can I use a manufacturer copay card if I have Medicare?
Usually no. Federal anti-kickback rules generally prohibit people with Medicare from using manufacturer copay coupons or savings cards. But you may still qualify for that manufacturer's Patient Assistance Program, which provides the drug free or low-cost based on income.
What are copay assistance foundations?
Nonprofits like PAN Foundation, HealthWell Foundation, Good Days, and Patient Advocate Foundation Co-Pay Relief give grants that cover out-of-pocket costs for specific diseases — and these grants can be used by people on Medicare.
How do I qualify?
Most programs use income limits (often a multiple of the federal poverty level), and many require a specific diagnosis. Manufacturer programs usually require you to be uninsured or underinsured. Each program lists its own rules.
Is it really free to apply?
Yes. Every program here is free to apply for. Be cautious of anyone charging a fee to enroll you in Extra Help, a foundation grant, or a manufacturer program — that's a red flag.
Can a Medicare agent help me apply?
Yes. Bret can screen you for Extra Help and Medicaid, point you to the right disease foundation, and make sure your Part D plan covers your medications for the lowest cost — at no charge.
